topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed to hum with malicious intent. Sarah stared at the quarterly projections on her screen, the numbers mocking her with their sheer audacity. A hushed conversation near the water cooler snagged her attention—whispers that solidified into a sickening realization. The acquisition. They were selling the whole operation. Her stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath her ribs. She slammed her laptop shut, the sharp crack echoing unnervingly in the quiet space. Every nerve ending felt frayed, buzzing with a frantic, trapped energy. She stood up, pacing the length of her desk, her jaw tight enough to crack enamel. "This is impossible," she muttered, the sound raspy. "They think we’re just inventory to be moved." She walked to the window, gripping the sill until her knuckles bleached white, watching the city below as if preparing for a siege.

[story 2]

Mark felt a slow, burning pressure building behind his eyes. He’d stumbled upon the internal memo tucked away in a shared drive—a cold, clinical document detailing the impending sale of Stellar Dynamics. When he read the final paragraph, the air seemed to evacuate his lungs. He didn't shout; instead, he let out a slow, deliberate breath that hitched in his chest. His hands clenched into fists at his sides, the tension radiating outward. He walked to the breakroom, the tile floor suddenly feeling too slick under his worn shoes. He leaned against the counter, staring blankly at a lukewarm coffee mug. "Are you serious?" he asked the empty room, his voice dangerously low, vibrating with suppressed intensity. He turned back to his desk, his movements jerky, as if his limbs were suddenly disconnected from his will. He began furiously highlighting every line in the document, underlining the betrayal in thick, angry strokes.

[story 3]

From the moment the CEO announced the restructuring, Elena knew something was profoundly wrong. She spent the next hour reviewing internal communications, piecing together fragments of a deception that tasted like ash in her mouth. When the final details of the sale materialized, the controlled composure she usually maintained shattered. Elena stood up so abruptly her chair scraped backward across the floor. A hot flush crept up her neck, staining her cheeks crimson. She began pacing the hallway, her footsteps heavy and uneven, each stride a declaration of utter disbelief and outrage. "They’re dismantling us," she hissed to the empty corridor, her voice tight with suppressed rage. She stopped by the water cooler, splashing cold water onto her face, trying to physically shock the mounting agitation out of her system, but the heat inside remained, a solid, immovable weight demanding release.
